summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's origins, philosophy, and growth on The Data Engineering Show. He explains how DuckDB emerged from CWI research as the first in-process analytical database, designed for universal deployment from phones to space satellites. He emphasizes DuckDB's focus on portability over hardware-specific optimization, explains why they built their own Parquet reader, and shares strong skepticism about AI's role in database internals while acknowledging its value for user-facing assistance like text-to-SQL.
key_points: DuckDB prioritizes universal portability over hardware-specific optimization, deliberately avoiding specialized CPU instructions to ensure it runs everywhere from IBM mainframes to Chinese homegrown CPUs
The biggest surprise was DuckDB's adoption as an embeddable component in data pipelines, not just as a standalone analytics tool
Building a competent Parquet reader requires query execution capabilities, meaning it inevitably becomes a database engine
AI has no place in core database engines—learned indexes are worse than traditional approaches, and ML-based query optimization suffers from overfitting and non-transferability
DuckDB's extension model enables radical extensibility (custom parsers, indexes, data types) that SaaS databases fundamentally cannot offer
money_quotes: I see AI on the same level of idiocy as Bitcoin personally.
Similar to Bitcoin having applications in stealing other people's money, AI has applications in rendering funny pictures for my slides.
If you're writing a parallel sort implementation, is an assistant going to help you? I have my serious doubts about that.
It feels like there's a gold rush and you sell shovels. Selling shovels is not glamorous. It is just a shop with shovels in it. But it earns a living.
In order to make a competent parquet reader, you do need query execution. There's just no way around it.
You wouldn't suggest that the Linux kernel should be taking system calls in natural language.
We really never, ever want to get killed by illegal instruction or anything like that, which even happened to NumPy a couple of months ago.
I personally really like the golden age of databases that I think we're in.
key_insight: DuckDB's power comes from radical simplicity and portability—avoiding hardware-specific optimizations and AI hype in favor of being a universal, embeddable database component that works everywhere.
